title: Machine Learning Section
text: Empowering students to build, deploy, and scale intelligent systems.

title: Machine Learning Section
text:
Welcome to the ML Section
This course provides a comprehensive introduction to Machine Learning, from fundamental concepts to advanced techniques. Whether you’re a complete beginner or looking to sharpen your skills, this section will guide you through the exciting world of AI.



Lecture 1

Introduction to ML


Understanding the basics, types of ML, and its impact on the modern world. We’ll set the stage for your ML journey.



Explore →




Lecture 2

Supervised Learning


Mastering regression and classification. Learn how to train models using labeled data and evaluate their performance.



Explore →




Lecture 3

Unsupervised Learning


Discovering patterns in unlabeled data. Dive into clustering, PCA, and anomaly detection techniques.



Explore →

title: Machine Learning Section
text:
Prerequisites
To get the most out of this course, we recommend:

Python Proficiency: Familiarity with basic syntax, data structures, and libraries like NumPy.
Mathematics: Basic understanding of linear algebra, calculus, and probability.
Curiosity: A desire to understand how data can be transformed into insights.

title: Lecture 2: Supervised Learning
text: Missing Semester CUET

title: Lecture 2: Supervised Learning
text:
What is Supervised Learning?

Training with labeled data.
The goal is to learn a mapping \(f(x) = y\).
\(x\) = features, \(y\) = target/label.

title: Lecture 2: Supervised Learning
text:
Linear Regression

Predicting continuous values.
Formula: \(y = mx + b\)




Code
import numpy as np
np.array([1, 2, 3])


array([1, 2, 3])

title: Lecture 2: Supervised Learning
text:
Logistic Regression

Binary classification (0 or 1).
Uses the Sigmoid function to map predictions to probabilities.

title: Lecture 2: Supervised Learning
text:
Decision Trees

Flowchart-like structure.
Easy to interpret and visualize.
Great for handling non-linear data.

title: Lecture 2: Supervised Learning
text:
Evaluation Metrics

Accuracy: Correct / Total
Precision: TP / (TP + FP)
Recall: TP / (TP + FN)

title: Lecture 2: Supervised Learning
text:
Questions?
Next up: Unsupervised Learning.

title: Lecture 1: Introduction to ML
text: By the end of this lecture, you will be able to: - Define Machine Learning and distinguish it from traditional programming. - Identify the three main types of ML: Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning. - Describe real-world applications of ML in various domains. - Understand the typical lifecycle of an ML project.

title: Lecture 1: Introduction to ML
text: By the end of this lecture, you will be able to: - Define Machine Learning and distinguish it from traditional programming. - Identify the three main types of ML: Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning. - Describe real-world applications of ML in various domains. - Understand the typical lifecycle of an ML project.

title: Lecture 1: Introduction to ML
text:
What is Machine Learning?
Machine Learning (ML) is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that focuses on building systems that can learn from data. Instead of being explicitly programmed with rules, ML models identify patterns and make decisions based on examples.

Traditional Programming vs. ML

Traditional Programming: Data + Rules \(\rightarrow\) Output
Machine Learning: Data + Output \(\rightarrow\) Rules (The Model)

title: Lecture 1: Introduction to ML
text:
Types of Machine Learning

Supervised Learning: Learning with labeled data (e.g., spam detection).
Unsupervised Learning: Finding hidden patterns in unlabeled data (e.g., customer segmentation).
Reinforcement Learning: Learning through trial and error to maximize rewards (e.g., game playing AI).

title: Lecture 1: Introduction to ML
text:
Real-World Applications

Healthcare: Disease diagnosis and drug discovery.
Finance: Fraud detection and algorithmic trading.
E-commerce: Recommendation systems (Amazon, Netflix).
Transportation: Self-driving cars and traffic prediction.

title: Lecture 1: Introduction to ML
text:
Course Roadmap
Throughout this section, we will cover: 1. Introduction & Fundamentals 2. Supervised Learning (Regression & Classification) 3. Unsupervised Learning (Clustering & PCA) 4. Model Evaluation & Deployment






TipPro Tip



Always start by understanding your data before choosing an ML model!

title: Lecture 3: Unsupervised Learning
text: Missing Semester CUET

title: Lecture 3: Unsupervised Learning
text:
What is Unsupervised Learning?

Learning from unlabeled data.
Finding hidden structure or patterns.
No “right answer” to compare against.

title: Lecture 3: Unsupervised Learning
text:
K-Means Clustering

Grouping data into \(K\) clusters.
Iterative algorithm:

Initialize centroids.
Assign points to nearest centroid.
Update centroids.





Code
print("Running K-Means simulation...")
print("Clusters converged after 5 iterations.")


Running K-Means simulation...
Clusters converged after 5 iterations.

title: Lecture 3: Unsupervised Learning
text:
PCA (Dimensionality Reduction)

Reducing features while keeping variance.
Visualizing high-dimensional data in 2D or 3D.

title: Lecture 3: Unsupervised Learning
text:
Anomaly Detection

Finding outliers in data.
Applications: Fraud, system failure, security.

title: Lecture 3: Unsupervised Learning
text:
Questions?
Thank you for participating in the ML Section!

title: Lecture 3: Unsupervised Learning
text: By the end of this lecture, you will be able to: - Define Unsupervised Learning and its primary goals. - Understand and implement K-Means Clustering. - Explain the concept of Dimensionality Reduction using PCA. - Identify use cases for Anomaly Detection. - Differentiate between various clustering techniques.

title: Lecture 3: Unsupervised Learning
text: By the end of this lecture, you will be able to: - Define Unsupervised Learning and its primary goals. - Understand and implement K-Means Clustering. - Explain the concept of Dimensionality Reduction using PCA. - Identify use cases for Anomaly Detection. - Differentiate between various clustering techniques.

title: Lecture 3: Unsupervised Learning
text:
What is Unsupervised Learning?
Unsupervised Learning involves training a model on data that has no labels. The model must find the underlying structure or distribution in the data on its own.

Key Tasks

Clustering: Grouping similar data points together (e.g., K-Means, Hierarchical Clustering).
Dimensionality Reduction: Reducing the number of random variables under consideration (e.g., PCA, t-SNE).
Association: Discovering rules that describe your data (e.g., people who buy X also buy Y).

title: Lecture 3: Unsupervised Learning
text:
Clustering Techniques

1. K-Means Clustering
Partitions the data into \(K\) clusters. Each data point belongs to the cluster with the nearest mean (centroid).


2. Hierarchical Clustering
Builds a hierarchy of clusters either through a top-down or bottom-up approach.

title: Lecture 3: Unsupervised Learning
text:
Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
PCA is a dimensionality reduction technique that transforms high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional form while retaining as much variance as possible. It’s often used for: - Data visualization - Noise reduction - Speeding up other ML algorithms

title: Lecture 3: Unsupervised Learning
text:
Anomaly Detection
Identifying rare items, events, or observations which raise suspicions by differing significantly from the majority of the data. Useful in: - Credit card fraud detection - Network security - Industrial equipment monitoring






NoteReflection



Think about how you could use Unsupervised Learning on a dataset of customer purchase history!

title: Lecture 1: Introduction to Machine Learning
text: Missing Semester CUET

title: Lecture 1: Introduction to Machine Learning
text:
What is Machine Learning?

A subset of AI that allows systems to learn from data.
Arthur Samuel (1959): “Field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed.”
Focus on statistical patterns and predictive modeling.

title: Lecture 1: Introduction to Machine Learning
text:
Types of ML

Supervised Learning: Mapping inputs to outputs using labeled data.
Unsupervised Learning: Discovering structure in unlabeled data.
Reinforcement Learning: Learning via rewards and penalties.

title: Lecture 1: Introduction to Machine Learning
text:
Applications in Real World

Image Recognition: FaceID, medical imaging.
NLP: Translation, sentiment analysis, chatbots.
Recommender Systems: Netflix, YouTube, Spotify.
Autonomous Vehicles: Self-driving cars.

title: Lecture 1: Introduction to Machine Learning
text:
Why Python for ML?
Python has a rich ecosystem of libraries: - NumPy: Numerical computing. - Pandas: Data manipulation. - Scikit-Learn: General ML tools. - PyTorch / TensorFlow: Deep Learning.



Code
print("Hello, ML World!")
print("Python version is ready for ML.")


Hello, ML World!
Python version is ready for ML.

title: Lecture 1: Introduction to Machine Learning
text:
Questions?
Feel free to ask about: - Course structure - Prerequisites - Career paths in ML

title: Lecture 2: Supervised Learning
text: By the end of this lecture, you will be able to: - Explain the concept of Supervised Learning. - Distinguish between Regression and Classification tasks. - Understand the basics of Linear and Logistic Regression. - Build and evaluate a simple Decision Tree model. - Use key evaluation metrics like Accuracy, Precision, and Recall.

title: Lecture 2: Supervised Learning
text: By the end of this lecture, you will be able to: - Explain the concept of Supervised Learning. - Distinguish between Regression and Classification tasks. - Understand the basics of Linear and Logistic Regression. - Build and evaluate a simple Decision Tree model. - Use key evaluation metrics like Accuracy, Precision, and Recall.

title: Lecture 2: Supervised Learning
text:
What is Supervised Learning?
Supervised Learning is the most common type of ML where the model is trained on a labeled dataset. A labeled dataset means that for every input, the correct output (or “label”) is already known.

Regression vs. Classification

Regression: Predicting a continuous numerical value (e.g., house prices, stock trends).
Classification: Predicting a discrete category or class (e.g., spam vs. not spam, cat vs. dog).

title: Lecture 2: Supervised Learning
text:
Key Algorithms

1. Linear Regression
Used for regression tasks. It finds the best-fit line that minimizes the difference between predicted and actual values.


2. Logistic Regression
Despite its name, it’s used for binary classification. It outputs the probability of an input belonging to a certain class.


3. Decision Trees
A tree-like model that makes decisions based on feature values. It’s intuitive and can handle both regression and classification.

title: Lecture 2: Supervised Learning
text:
Evaluation Metrics
To know how well our model is performing, we use: - Mean Squared Error (MSE): For regression. - Accuracy: Percentage of correct predictions. - Precision & Recall: Critical for imbalanced datasets. - F1-Score: The harmonic mean of precision and recall.






WarningOverfitting



Be careful of overfitting! A model that performs perfectly on training data but poorly on new data is not useful in production.
